AGREEMENT FOR SERVICES

On September 15, 1989, CONTRA COSTA WATER DISTRICT,
a local governmental entity (hereinafter "CCWD"), and HASSELTINE
BEST, a California general partnership comprised by Eric H. Hasseltine
and Linda P. Best (hereinafter "HB"), agreed as follows:

1. HB shall perform for CCWD the services described in section
2 hereof as are from time to time requested by CCWD's General
Manager or his delegee (hereinafter "the General Manager"). The
services shall be in accordance with the directions, under the control,
and to the satisfaction of the General Manager

2. The services to be performed by HB shall be related to the
accomplishment of CCWD's Los Vaqueros Project, the acquisition of
real property, and the furnishing of water from the Project to
potential service areas. The services shall include the representation
of CCWD at meetings of public entities and private organizations. HB
shall also meet and confer with CCWD's staff and consultants as
necessary.

3. CCWD shall pay HB $150.00 per hour spent in the
performance of this agreement upon receipt of monthly statements
from HB. CCWD shall reimburse HB for extraordinary expenses
incurred in connection with this agreement that are approved in
advance by the General Manager.

4. CCWD shall furnish to HB without cost such data, reports,
drawings, and the like which are readily available to CCWD and shall
provide engineering and drafting services for HB to the extent the
General Manager determines that they are reasonable and necessary.

5. The relationship between the parties shall be that of
independent contractors and not of employer-employee.

6. The terms of this agreement shall be for one year from the
date hereof and for such additional term as the parties may agree
upon, unless sooner terminated. This agreement may be terminated

1

at any time by the General Manager or a partner of HB effective
upon delivery of a written notice of termination to the other party.

AGREEMENT
FOR
CONSULTING SERVICES


This AGREEMENT is between Contra Costa Water District, a local
governmental entity ("Client"), and McGill-Martin-Self, a general
partnership ("Consultant").

SCOPE OF SERVICES

Consultant shall furnish those services described under the
heading Phase II Land Use Studies and Future Water Service
Areas and Facilities, Exhibit "A" hereto, within 22 weeks
after execution of this agreement by Client.

COMPENSATION

Consultant shall be compensated on a time and material basis
in accordance with the rates and charges set forth in
Exhibit "B" hereto, provided that the total compensation
incurred shall not exceed $74,000 without prior written
approval of Client. The final payment portion consisting of
not less than 10% of the total compensation shall be
withheld until Client review and approval of all Resulting
Products.

INVOICES

To assist Client in tracking costs, monthly invoices will be
issued by Consultant for all work performed under this
agreement. Such invoices will be due and payable upon
completion of the services described herein. Consultant
shall maintain accounting records in accordance with
generally accepted accounting principles and practices to
substantiate all invoiced records. These records will be
available for review by Client for a period of one year
after submission of Consultant's final invoice hereunder.

OWNERSHIP OF PROJECT DOCUMENTS

All files, records, documents, equipment, computer programs,
computer data and related items shall remain the exclusive
property of the Client whether provided by the Client or
prepared by Consultant or others on behalf of the Client
under this Agreement. The services provided for in this
Agreement are for the exclusive use of the Client and Client
shall without limitation be entitled to utilize the data and
other information to be provided to Client as described in
the Scope of Services in any way without any payments to
Consultant other than those specified in this agreement.

TERMINATION

This Agreement may be terminated for cause, in whole or in
part, if either party fails substantially to perform through
no fault of the other and does not commence correction of
such nonperformance within five days of written notice and
diligently complete the correction thereafter.

Client may also terminate this Agreement, in whole or in
part, if Client has a reasonable basis for termination,
including but not limited to major changes in the work or
project requirements, cancellation of the project, loss of
outside funding, or failure of the Board of Directors to
authorize funding in subsequent fiscal years, whether
Consultant has failed to substantially perform under this
agreement.

No such termination shall be effective unless the other
party is given: (1) not less than ten (10) calendar days
prior to be written notice of the intent to terminate; and (2)
a reasonable opportunity to consult with the terminating
party.

Upon receipt of a termination notice, Consultant shall: (1)
promptly discontinue all affected services unless Client
notifies otherwise; and (2) deliver or otherwise make
available to Client all data, drawings, specifications,
reports, estimates, summaries, and such other information
and materials as Consultant may have accumulated in
performing this Agreement, whether completed or in progress.

Upon termination, Consultant will be paid for amounts due
and not previously paid to the Consultant for services
completed or in progress at the time of termination in
accordance with the Agreement, and for services completed
after termination at the request of the Client and any
reasonable cost incidental to the termination of services.
Such payments shall not include costs related to lost profit
associated with the expected completion of the work.

2

INDEMNIFICATION

A).  Consultant agrees to indemnify and defend Client from
any loss, cost, or expense claimed by third parties for
property damage and bodily injury, including death,
caused solely by the negligence or willful misconduct
of Consultant, its employees, or agents in connection
with the Project.

B).  Client agrees to indemnify and defend Consultant from
any loss, cost, or expense claimed by third parties
for property damage and bodily injury, including death,
caused solely by the negligence or willful misconduct
of Client, its employees, or agents in connection with
the Project.

C).  If the negligence or willful misconduct of both the
Consultant and Client (or a person identified above
for whom each is liable) is a cause of such, damage or
injury, the loss, cost or expense shall be shared
between Consultant and Client in proportion to their
relative degrees of negligence or willful misconduct
and the right of indemnity shall apply for such
proportion.

INSURANCE

The Consultant will maintain throughout this Agreement the
following insurance, and shall submit Certificates of
Insurance thereto upon request by Client. The Client also
reserves the right to require the Consultant to provide
Reservee Insurance Policies for review by Client.

A).  Worker's compensation and employer's liability
insurance as required by the State of California.
Consultant shall require any subcontractor to provide
it with evidence of such insurance as required by the
State of California.

B).  Consultant shall take out and maintain during the life
of the Agreement, professional liability insurance with
a minimum of $1,000,000 of liability, with a maximum
$100,000 deductible. Consultant shall furnish a
Certificate of Insurance to the Client. The policy
will provide 30 days' written notice to the District
for cancellation of any material alteration.

C).  Consultant shall take out and maintain during the life
of the Agreement Comprehensive Automobile and General
Liability Insurance that provides protection from
claims which may arise from operations or performance
under this Agreement.

3

The amounts of insurance shall not less than the following:

Single Limit Coverage applying to Bodily and Personal
Injury Liability or Property Damage $1,000,000.

The following endorsements must be indicated on the
certificate:

1)   Client, its Directors, Officers, and employees are
     Additional Insureds in the policy as to the work
     being performed under the agreement;

2)   The coverage is Primary and no other insurance
     carried by the Client will be called upon to
     contribute to a loss under this coverage;

3)   The policy covers Blanket Contractual Liability;

4)   The policy limits of liability area provided on
     Occurrence basis;

5)   The policy covers broad form Property Damage
     Liability;

6)   The policy covers Personal Injury as well as
     Bodily Injury Liability;

7)   The policy covers Explosion, Collapse, and
     Underground hazards;

8)   The policy covers Products and Completed
     Operations;

9)   The coverage shall not be cancelled nor
     materially altered unless 30 days' written notice
     is given to the Client.

D).  Consultant shall be responsible for requiring
     insurance coverage by subconsultants and the
     Consultant shall determine the amount and adequacy of
     such coverage. Within 30 days after the Notice to
     Proceed, the Consultant shall submit to the Client for
     its information a list of the public liability and
     other insurance coverages provided by each
     subconsultant, including the amount of such coverage
     and the insurers.

LIMITATION OF LIABILITY

     To the maximum extent permitted by the law, Consultant's
liability for Client's damages will not exceed the limits of
insurance provided by Consultant in accordance with
paragraph 8.

4

ADDITIONAL PROVISIONS

Additional provisions of this contract are set forth in
Exhibit "C".

EXHIBIT A

PHASE II

LAND USE STUDIES AND FUTURE

WATER SERVICE AREAS AND FACILITIES

PHASE II - LAND USE STUDIES AND FUTURE WATER SERVICE AREAS AND
FACILITIES

A.  PURPOSE

    Phase I products show known constraints which may impact future
    sizing and siting of CCWD systems. Phase II will further analyze
    the impacts of these constraints as well as known land
    densities to aid in the analysis and future growth projection of
    the area. Specifically, Phase II of this study will identify and
    analyze:

        1. Development impacts and land use density and population
           projections from existing and proposed general plans, specific
           plans, EIR's and studies which may affect the study area.

        2. Expansion potential of facilities and areas as provided
           through discussion with major public agencies as well as
           service and utility districts, which may impact CCWD's
           long-range planning goals and needs.

        3. Coordination with the Los Vaqueros project for interim
           and ultimate water demand projections and joint Water
           Treatment Plant phasing.

        4. Future growth areas and potential land use distribution;
           three land use scenarios, based upon the following, will
           be identified:

               a. Existing land use and approved projects.

               b. Current general plans and/or Draft
                  County General Plan.

               c. Expanded growth alternates (with and without
                  agricultural core will be studied).

    McGill-Martin-Self will meet with CCWD to provide assumptions and
    parameters for the "Expanded Growth"Alternatives"prior to initiating
    population projection assumptions, planning horizons and/or build-
    out conditions. (Two meetings). A summary of this information
    will be presented to CCWD in memorandum form for their review.

B.  WORK SCOPE FOR EAST COUNTY STUDY AREA

    Estimated Fee: Not to exceed $60,000

    Once the above information has been identified, analyzed and
    reviewed by CCWD, McGill-Martin-Self will:

Utilize composite constraints/data exhibit map prepared in the
Phase I portion of this Study which identifies areas where
development may occur.

2. Prepare three land use maps based on the three land use
scenarios identified previously (Item A-4) incorporating
limitations for development from the composite
constraints/data exhibit map.

3. Develop one plan for future treated water service areas
as well as coordination with the Los Vaqueros project
team (Task 16). A brief summary stating assumptions for
each land use scenario, as well as assumptions for
interim or permanent agricultural water supplies will be
provided by McGill-Martin-Self.

4. Work with CCWD to develop a list of potential facilities,
corridors, canals, etc. for servicing future
needs/demands. (Two meetings).

5. Identify potential siting of future CCWD facilities and
corridors as well as points of delivery; meet with CCWD
to discuss potential siting locations. (Two meetings).

6. Meet with CCWD staff to discuss the development of
service agreements with local agencies and committees.
(Two meetings).

C. WORKSCOPE FOR NEROLY - CLAYTON TREATED WATER SERVICES ROUTE

Estimated Fee: Not to exceed $10,000

The proposed route will start approximately 6,000 feet north of
Lone Tree Way and 1,500 feet west of Empire Road at the site of
the proposed Neroly Water Treatment Plant. The route runs
southerly to the intersection of Walnut Boulevard and Marsh Creek
Road, from there the route runs along Marsh Creek Road to the
west ending in Clayton at the intersection of Clayton Road and
Ygnacio Valley Road. The length of the route is estimated to be
approximately 27 miles. The pipe will be under pressure and will
require a minimum of three feet of soil cover. We understand that
the trench excavated for the pipe will be approximately six feet
wide by seven feet deep. The portion of the route along Marsh
Creek Road will probably be located in the existing public road
right-of-ways as much as possible. However, the portion of the
route north of Marsh Creek Road will cross undeveloped land.

It is our intent to identify and locate areas of potential
geological and geotechnical hazards along the route. A partial
list of potential hazards which we will be analyzing includes:

■ landslides,
■ unstable slopes,
■ erosion hazards,
■ hard rock requiring drilling
and/or blasting,

■ land subsidence,
■ active earthquake faults,
■ other earthquake-related hazards,
■ soft ground requiring extra
support during construction.

The study will incorporate existing geotechnical data provided
by    CCWD for the Los Vaqueros Project pipeline from the proposed
Neroly Treatment Plant to March Creek Road and additional
geotechnical information used in connection with a pipeline project
from Clayton to the Royal Oaks Golf Course. These data will be
reviewed with respect to the project requirements and up dated, if
necessary.

The proposed scope of work for this portion of the Phase II study
is:

1. Aerial photographs of the proposed route will be studied
and geological interpretations will be made of features
observed on the photographs. Aerial photographs from the
library of Pacific Aerial Survey will be obtained.
The photographs will be analyzed and evaluated by the
engineering geologist. Geologic features along the route
will be identified and located. In particular, land-
slides will be identified by the aerial photograph study.

2. Geologic and geotechnical information from public and
private sources will be examined. Geological maps and
other geologic publications for the area will be analyzed
and known geologic features along the route will be
identified and located.

3. After the aerial photograph interpretation and geological
research is completed, the engineering geologist will
make a field reconnaissance along those portions of the
proposed route where significant geological hazards have
been identified. The geological hazards will be
evaluated in the field.

4. At the conclusion of our study, we will submit a report
which describes our investigation and presents our
findings, conclusions and recommendations regarding the
potential geologic and geotechnical hazards along the
proposed pipeline route. Recommendations may include
suggestions for relocating portions of the route and may
include conceptual design recommendations for mitigating
the effects of some of the hazards.

5. Meet with CCWD staff three times to discuss status of
this portion of the Phase II study.

D. RESULTING PRODUCTS

Phase II services and studies will require a great deal of
interaction between McGill-Martin-Self, their consultants, and
CCWD to ensure that proper direction and progress is made. The
primary goal of Phase II will be to prepare population projections
to identify CCWD facility and service needs as well as the

location and sizing of those needs. As the County continues
to grow and develop, this study will help CCWD understand
and anticipate that growth to meet long-range water service
needs.

     1.     A refined plan consisting of future treated water
            service areas, major pipeline corridors (pipelines
            greater than 18" in diameter) and related water
            delivery facilities.

     2.     Provide a Summary Report of Phase II work,
            including a brief land use summary of potential
            growth areas, impacts of growth on future facility
            siting and sizing, and a general phasing program
            for the development of a treated water supply
            system in East County.

     3.     Provide a brief summary of the feasibility and
            constraints for the location of the Neroly/Clayton
            route as it relates to geotechnical constraints.
            (See Item C4.)

E.   FEES AND PAYMENT SCHEDULE

     In accordance with the not to exceed total compensation of
$74,000, McGill-Martin-Self will work on time and materials
bases. Consultant shall monitor project costs at all times.
Should the project costs begin to exceed the budgeted
amounts for that particular stage of work, Consultant shall
meet with Client to resolve future expenditure and work
effort within the budgeted amount.

     Client shall pay the expense of checking and inspection
fees, zoning and annexation application fees, assessment
fees, soil engineering fees, soil testing fees, aerial
topography fees, and all other fees, permits, bond premiums,
title company charges, mileage, blueprints and
reproductions, and all other charges not specifically
covered by the terms of this agreement. Estimated fee
for incurred expenses is to not exceed $4,000.

     Payment will be made by CCWD within twenty (20) days of
receipt of invoice except for final payment as stipulated
under compensation on page 1 of this Agreement.

     Billing will be submitted to CCWD on a monthly basis based
on time expended in accordance with the hourly charge rate
schedule attached (Exhibit "B"). All billings made on the
basis of hourly charge rates will include an accounting of
hours worked for each item of services and be submitted
separate from services provided on any other basis.

Dear Mr. Seegmiller:

On April 10, 1991, you sent a letter to our client, Mike
McGill of McGill-Martin-Self Engineering, asking him to complete
a Statement of Economic Interests (MSRI; FPPC Form 730) as
required by the Political Reform Act. I discussed this request
with Bill Woska on April 18, who clarified that you believe Mr.
McGill comes under the definition of "consultant" contained in 2
California Code of Regulations section 18700.

As you know, our firm has advised the Contra Costa County
Water District ("CCWD") in the past on political law matters. We
would therefore not provide Mr. McGill with our interpretation of
the Act vis-a-vis his duties with the CCWD without your
permission. We understand that the CCWD does not object to our
communication with Mr. McGill on this matter; this letter thus
summarizes our analysis of the situation.

We respectfully disagree with your conclusion and believe
that Mr. McGill does not meet the Fair Political Practices
Commission's definition of consultant and does not need to file
an SEI. Though Mr. McGill does provide consulting services to
the CCWD, we believe that his duties place him under the
exceptions of Regulation 18700. We request that you consider the
following information in this regard.

The Regulation defines consultant as anyone who, under
contract to a public agency, provides "information, advice,
recommendation or counsel" to the agency. A person is not a
consultant, however, if he or she works "independent of the
control or direction of the agency, other than normal contract
monitoring" and possesses no decision-making authority. (See
copy enclosed.)

Mr. Ed Seegmiller
April 22, 1991
Page 2

The FPPC has consistently advised consultants who come under
this exception that they do not have disclosure or
disqualification rules under the Act. In In re Maloney (1977) 3
FPPC Opns. 69, the FPPC concluded that a surveyor/engineer under
contract with Glenn County was not a consultant, reasoning that:

The preparation of surveys and engineering
studies would appear to fall within this
exclusion. When performing these services,
the county surveyor-engineer is not involved
in any official decision making. He is
merely carrying out the terms of a contract
just as any vendor of goods or services to
the county might. He is not subject to the
control or discretion of the county when he
performs his work, but is governed by the
provisions of his contract.

(See also FPPC Advice Letter to James Corn (4/12/90) No. I-89-433
[civil engineers who provide designs for and estimate costs of
district improvement projects do not need to file SEIs) (copy
enclosed); Advice Letter to Carolyn Crouch (7/22/83) No. A-83-069
[EIR consultants do not need to file SEIs because they only
delivered products] (copy enclosed).) The FPPC has also
drawn a distinction between "consulting engineers" (who usually
have no disclosure obligations) and "project engineers" (who
usually must file SEIs). (Advice Letter to Vera Todorov
(10/12/90) No. I-90-440 (copy enclosed).)

On the other hand, the FPPC has instructed consultants to
file SEIs when they function as additional staff to the agency,
under the agency's direction and control. (See, e.g., Advice
Letter to Peter Greenwald (5/13/90) No. A-90-349 [SCAQMD followed
consultants' recommendations without substantive review.]; Advice
Letter to David Kaplan (6/8/82) No. A-82-108 [consultants worked
in agency offices and provided input into agency projects on a
regular basis] (copies enclosed).)

Mr. McGill appears to come under the exception to the
definition of consultant in Regulation 18700. His duties are
similar to those of the surveyors in the Maloney opinion and the
engineers and consultants in the Corn, Crouch and Todorov Advice
Letters, in that he prepares his maps, studies and reports
independent of that CCWD's control. Moreover, he has no
authority to make final decisions on CCWD plans and projects.
